text: I usually have something simple, like toast with scrambled eggs or muesli with yogurt and fruit. Sometimes, if I'm feeling fancy, I'll make some full English breakfast – bacon, sausages, tomatoes, mushrooms... You know, all the basics. But most days, it's just something easy-peasy that gives me enough energy to tackle whatever the day brings. My grandkids always say my cooking is better because I make them tea sandwiches for elevenses!

I usually have a simple sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner, perhaps some fruit or an apple. Sometimes, my grandkids like to join me for lunch; we'll share what we're having together. We might watch something on television while we eat – probably the news or one of our favorite programs. Afternoon tea is more of a treat, maybe scones and a cuppa, before settling into my usual afternoon slump.
I'm a bit of an early bird, so I try to be in bed by 10 o'clock most nights. It helps me feel rested and refreshed for the morning, which is my favorite part of the day!

I find a good cuppa and some gentle stretching before starting my day really sets me up for a productive morning. It's amazing how much more alert I feel afterwards. Then, I try to fit in some gardening – even if it's just tidying up the veg patch or watering my roses. There's something therapeutic about being outdoors and watching everything come alive. Of course, after all that fresh air, I'm ready for a nice warm breakfast and maybe some toast and jam while catching up on the radio. A bit of music, too, always helps create a pleasant atmosphere. As an afternoon dips in energy, I usually take a little siesta, curl up with a good book or watch a nature documentary on telly. When the weather permits, I love taking a leisurely walk along the canal towpath nearby; there's nothing like getting out into nature to recharge those batteries! And, of course, making sure I have enough tea in the pot is essential – priorities, eh?! All in all, a simple yet satisfying way to look after myself, thank goodness we live so far away from city noise and chaos now...

I'd say my networking style is quite informal, really. It's more a case of knowing who to turn to when needed rather than actively chasing connections. In my previous roles, I was part of various committees and groups focused on different aspects of our work – education and healthcare administration. Those were great opportunities to meet like-minded people and build relationships based on shared responsibilities. Now, since retiring, I try to make the most of those connections while still being mindful of keeping them professional. Occasionally, we have little gatherings at the local community center where colleagues and former students gather for refreshments; these are always lovely occasions to catch up and reminisce about old times. My son helps me attend some of these so we're not too far out of pocket financially. It feels much better now, though, having had all those years of building trust and friendships before moving into retirement. Sometimes, I think some younger folks might find networking intimidating, but once you know what's expected, it becomes easier. Of course, there will be moments when someone asks how to improve their job performance, which makes me reflect upon my past...

I have found a simple system works quite well for me. Since my peak productivity is between 8am and 12pm, I try to plan most of my work during those hours. I dedicate myself to focused work without any distractions, like turning off notifications on my phone or closing unnecessary tabs on my computer. After lunch, I take an hour break before resuming some tasks or switching gears into more relaxed projects that need attention. This breaks helps prevent burnout and allows me to recharge for the rest of the morning. It suits me being able to be efficient while still taking breaks whenever needed.

I'm rather fond of video calls, actually. It allows me to catch up with my grandkids who live far away and be there virtually whenever we need to talk. My granddaughter is quite tech-savvy and has been very helpful in setting everything up for us. We usually stick to regular phone-style conversations, so I only join in occasionally to add a bit of visual chat – like showing them what I'm making while I bake! But yes, video calls have become an essential part of staying connected with loved ones across distances.

I think that's a great question! You know, people always say retirement is all about freedom and flexibility, but sometimes it takes some getting used to, doesn't it? For me, staying motivated has been about finding my rhythm again. Since retiring, I have more control over how I spend my time, which can be lovely, but it also makes me feel like I'm drifting. To combat that, I try setting little personal targets for myself each morning before bed – perhaps finishing a certain chapter in a book, tending to my garden, or simply enjoying a nice cuppa while watching the birds outside. Having something to look forward to gives me a sense of purpose. And, of course, spending time with my grandkids and loved ones helps too! It's funny, I find I miss the regular routine of an office job, though. It was so satisfying knowing what needed doing every day. Now, I need to remind myself why these daily routines were there in the first place. Reminding myself that I still have responsibilities to others, even if they're not work-related, keeps me going. Plus, having a bit of free time allows me to take breaks whenever I need them, which isn't always easy for someone who thrives on being busy! Lastly

I think changing priorities is quite normal, especially in an admin role like mine where circumstances always seem to shift. What I find helpful is having a bit of flexibility built into my workload so that I'm prepared for sudden changes. This way, even when something else comes along, I can adjust accordingly without feeling too flustered. It really depends on what the priority shift entails, though. If someone asks me to take on more responsibilities suddenly – which does happen – I'll make sure I have all the information I need before diving in. Sometimes we'd discuss it with our manager beforehand to agree on expectations. Other times, it might be a last-minute decision; either way, clarity helps avoid confusion. Ultimately, being able to adapt and prioritize effectively has been key throughout my career. It shows I'm committed to delivering results while managing competing demands.

I think advocacy is an essential part of being a strong communicator and effective admin assistant. When it comes to advocating for myself at work, I'd say it usually boils down to speaking clearly and confidently about my capabilities and any concerns I may have. As someone who prides herself on her organization and attention to detail, I try to be proactive rather than reactive – if something isn't quite right, I'll speak up and suggest solutions rather than letting issues slide. For instance, during my time at the GP surgery where I worked, there were instances where we had too many patients waiting to be seen due to understaffing. Instead of simply accepting it was out of control, I offered some suggestions like reorganising our scheduling system or even proposing temporary assistance from other trusts. My boss appreciated these contributions, which helped streamline processes and reduce wait times. It really came down to having confidence in what I knew best – the inner workings of the office and how we could improve operations more efficiently. Being assertive yet considerate has always served me well; finding that balance allows me to communicate effectively while still showing respect for others' opinions and decisions. And ultimately, getting results helps build trust among colleagues!

When I feel like I'm being misunderstood, my first instinct is usually to take a step back, breathe deeply, and try to rephrase what I mean. Sometimes, it's because people assume something based on limited information, so clarifying my thoughts helps me make sure everyone gets the message across correctly. If necessary, I'll ask them to explain how they understood what I said, which usually gives us an opportunity to have a more productive conversation. It's always better to resolve any misunderstandings before they escalate into bigger issues, isn't it? It reminds me of a saying my grandmother used to say – "patience and curiosity get you further than rushing through life." When we stop and listen actively, we're more likely to grasp each other's perspective accurately. So, taking a moment to reflect and communicate openly usually does wonders!

I love being surrounded by my loved ones! When we have family gatherings, I usually spend most of the time chatting with everyone, catching up on each other's lives. My grandkids always seem to be getting into something mischievous, so I try to keep a close eye on them while still having a conversation with others. We tend to cook meals together, either one of us brings our favourite dish to share, which is lovely because there's always plenty to eat. Sometimes we'll play games like Scrabble or cards afterwards – those moments make me chuckle every time! When it comes to activities, I'm happy to join in, whether it's going for a walk, watching a movie, or playing board games. We all generally stick together until bedtime; sometimes I might sneak away quietly to finish some knitting or read a bit before bed myself. Family gatherings really mean a lot to me, even though my husband isn't here anymore. It makes me feel his presence in some way.

I'm afraid I find all these new-fangled platforms a bit overwhelming, to be honest. As someone who's used to more traditional methods like writing letters or word-of-mouth, I struggle to keep track of what everyone is saying and why. My son says I should really sort out my profile on LinkedIn, but I haven't got round to it yet. To me, there seems too much emphasis on promoting oneself rather than genuinely connecting with others. Give me a cuppa and some face-to-face conversation any day! If people need information, I'll send them an email or give them a ring – that way we can have a proper chat.

I think I'd always try to be honest and open, but gentle, too. You know, straight-forward is usually best, but sometimes tactful words can go a lot further. It depends on who we're talking to, really. With close family members like my grandkids, I'm more relaxed, but with colleagues or strangers, I might take a moment to consider how they'll react before sharing something that could upset them. But ultimately, being kind and genuinely interested in what they have to say helps me navigate tricky conversations, whether it's discussing health issues or, occasionally, difficult work-related matters.

I'd say I'm quite a straight shooter, so I tend to speak my mind and be honest, even if others might think differently. If someone tries to influence me into something I'm not entirely sure is best, I'll usually politely explain why I feel that way, while still being considerate of their thoughts. It's essential to find common ground, isn't it? We all have our own strengths and weaknesses; sometimes we need each other's input. But once I make up my mind, I try to stick to it unless there's compelling reason to reconsider – like strong evidence or overwhelming advice from those closer to the situation.

To be honest, I find those really annoying. I try to avoid them altogether, especially on websites where I know I'm looking at something specific like a recipe site or a gardening forum. If an ad comes on screen while I'm browsing, I usually close it immediately. My granddaughter says I should join some sort of ad-blocking service, so maybe I'll look into one of those. But honestly, I'd much rather just have peace and quiet while I browse!

To be honest, I find managing multiple browser tabs quite overwhelming these days. My granddaughter helps me out a lot with setting everything up and showing me how to switch between them efficiently. However, I still prefer keeping my most used websites pinned to the top so I'm sure which one is open where. Usually, I try to limit myself to only what I need open – like news, emails, and maybe YouTube for some calming nature documentaries. It gets chaotic enough already!

I try not to engage with them too much, to be honest. As a widow who's lost loved ones myself, I find it quite hurtful when people feel the need to lash out at others online. When I come across something nasty or unfair, I usually take a deep breath and remind myself that it says more about the other person than me. If someone decides to share an abusive comment, I block them straight away. My grandkids always say how mean some people can be online, and I think they're spot on. You can't control what others post, so why bother engaging with it? Just focus on spreading kindness and positivity instead!

I usually end up calling my grandson, who's very handy with computers, for assistance. He takes a bit of explaining what went wrong and we manage to sort out whatever issue there is. Sometimes he has to walk me through updating something himself before I'm able to proceed. It's always quite embarrassing having these sorts of conversations! But his tech-savvy nature makes all the difference.

I make a list every month of all my income and expenses, including any regular subscriptions like utility bills, council tax, and insurance premiums. Then I sort them into categories, such as essential costs like rent/mortgage, food, transport, and savings targets. Once everything is listed out, I work out how much I have available each month and allocate it accordingly. For example, I try to pay most essentials first – like rent and utilities – before moving onto other monthly payments. If there's still money left over, I put some towards saving for the future or specific purposes, like grandkids' birthdays or holidays. It might sound simple, but keeping track helps me avoid overspending and ensures I'm making progress towards my priorities. Plus, having a set system makes managing finances feel more manageable and gives me peace of mind knowing everything is taken care of.

I try to think about whether I really need something before signing up for a subscription service. If I haven't used it in months, probably isn't going to be much use anymore. Or maybe there's another way I could achieve what I'm looking for that wouldn't involve paying extra money each month. Sometimes my grandkids will tell me I should cancel some of these things because I never ask them any more, so perhaps that's a sign it was one of those disposable extras! At least now I have all our family photos sorted into albums instead, thanks to their tech expertise – saves me having to dig out old photo albums every time we meet. But generally, I find making an honest assessment helps me make decisions like that.

I'm a bit of a thrifty soul, even now that I'm fully retired! When considering big-ticket purchases, like replacing my washing machine or taking a coach trip to the Lake District with Joan, we always try to be sensible and look out our options before making a decision. We'll weigh the costs against what we really need versus what we'd simply like to have. If it's something essential, like replacing an appliance, we might opt for the most affordable option available. On the other hand, for experiences like holidays, we're happy to splurge a little more because those memories will last longer. But overall, I think being frugal has been instilled in me since childhood – waste not, want not, you know!

I'd always say buy second-hand whenever possible. We didn't have all these fancy sales channels like we do now when I was younger, so my husband and I were taught to make do with what we had. It wasn't a matter of being cheap; it was just a case of making ends meet. And besides, there's something satisfying about finding a bargain and giving an item a new lease on life. Now, of course, I'm more aware of the environmental benefits too – every little helps! Still, if someone offers me a decent deal on something modern and efficient, I'll consider it. But generally speaking...second-hand first, please!

Well, I think it's just a matter of being mindful of one's spending habits and sticking to what we've always done, really. My husband used to say "a penny saved is a penny earned", and I still believe that today. We're definite savers, even though we haven't had much disposable income since he passed away. It's all been managed carefully, so far. I make sure to keep track of our expenses and try not to impulse-buy – although I must admit there have been moments where my grandkids have tempted me into buying something! Still, we stick to our budgets and plan ahead. The key is knowing how much we can afford, and making conscious decisions about what's truly necessary versus nice-to-haves.

I try to take advantage of them whenever I can! You know how we always say "use what you buy"? My mum used to tell me that too. It's a habit I never really broke. When there are discounts on staples like toiletries, cleaning supplies, or clothing, I make sure to stock up while I can. And of course, when it comes to bigger items like furniture or electronics, I'll usually wait until the sale is even deeper before making a purchase. It's all about being sensible and saving those pennies, isn't it? Saves us a pretty penny in the end, and I think it's worth the effort. Plus, I love seeing everything laid out so neatly in the shop – makes shopping feel more enjoyable, somehow.

I think portion control is essential for a number of reasons, including keeping an eye on one's figure, ensuring you're getting enough nutrients while avoiding excessive calories, and being mindful of your spending habits. You know how it is – too much of a good thing isn't always better! A little bit goes a long way. When I'm cooking for myself, I try to measure out ingredients so I have precise amounts; I find that helps me avoid going overboard and saves money, which is always welcome. In my household, we like to bake together with our grandkids, making sure everyone has fun and gets involved. They love measuring cups and spoons, and it teaches them valuable lessons about measurements and following instructions. We stick to tried-and-tested recipes that serve a reasonable size, usually two portions maximum. It makes sense to save leftovers instead of letting food go to waste, don't you agree? It's all about balance, really. Indulging every now and then never hurts, but regularly gobbling everything in sight does lead to unwanted weight gain. My late husband used to say, "Moderation is key," and I still believe he was spot on. So, yes, controlling portions simply seems common sense to me. Would you agree?

I think a gentle, straightforward conversation is best. None of that jumping-in-the-deep-end sort of thing, thank you very much! You know, someone who understands what's bothering me will tell me exactly where I am going wrong so we can work together to fix it. I'd rather have an open, honest discussion than some psycho-babble nonsense. My late husband used to say, "Just talk it out, love," and you're right – most issues aren't rocket science. A bit of common sense, kindness, and shared perspective usually sorts everything out in the end. Plus, I'm always keen to be aware of my thoughts and feelings; being introspective has helped me become more self-assured throughout the years. Now, let's sit down, put our feet up, and chat like proper friends.

Preventing burnout is a constant concern for me now that my son has grown older and takes more responsibility for his daily life. You know, I used to be quite busy myself what with running a household and taking care of three young ones, so I'm sure many people find themselves in similar circumstances. I think the key is finding a balance between being productive and allowing yourself some downtime. As someone who likes to have a structured day, I make sure to plan out my morning until mid-afternoon, focusing on the most critical tasks. Afternoons tend to take a bit of an energy hit, which makes sense given all the hard work we put into our bodies during the first part of the day. If I feel myself starting to flag, I try to step outside for a quick walk – even if it's just round the block. Getting fresh air always seems to refresh me, reminds me of nature's beauty, and helps me refocus. Sometimes, I'll treat myself to something nice afterwards, like a cup of tea or a slice of cake, too! It's funny how those little indulgences can lift your spirits. Another thing I remind myself regularly is that there will always be tomorrow. When I start feeling overwhelmed, I pause and ask whether today

I think a regular bedtime routine helps me wind down, so I try going to bed around 10 pm every night. It's become quite habitual now. Beforehand, I might read something calming like a historical novel or listen to some soothing music – nothing too stimulating. A warm cup of herbal tea is nice before bed too. After that, I aim to be asleep within an hour. I know getting enough rest is essential for staying sharp during my morning walks, which kick-start my day nicely!

I try to mix it up a bit, even though my body isn't what it used to be! Since I'm more energetic in the morning, I usually start with some gentle stretching and yoga exercises before breakfast. On Tuesdays and Thursdays, I join a local fitness class specifically designed for seniors – we walk while chatting and having a laugh together. When the weather permits, I love spending time in our backyard tending to my vegetable patch and herbs; there's something therapeutic about getting out among nature. Occasionally, I'll take a brisk 20-minute stroll along the nearby canal path. As for other forms of exercise, I have to admit that I haven't been too keen on gym work since retirement... Still, every now and then, I might treat myself to an invigorating bike ride with one of my grandkids. Nothing beats the joy of sharing those moments together! It's all about balance, really – keeping my mind and body active is essential to staying happy and feeling fulfilled. And let me tell you, having a sense of purpose helps so much!

I think I'm a fairly methodical person, which helps me weigh my options quite thoroughly. When faced with decisions, I like to consider all aspects of each choice, taking into account what might work best for me now, how it'll affect my daily life, and whether it aligns with my priorities. Sometimes I find myself thinking about worst-case scenarios too – imagining the least pleasant outcomes to make sure I cover everything. Once I have a good idea of the pros and cons, I try to trust my instincts; sometimes there isn't one 'right' option, so it comes down to choosing the one that feels most suitable given the circumstances.

I think incorporating feedback is a vital part of making improvements and ensuring what we're doing is effective. For me, taking feedback has always been about being open to constructive criticism, considering other people's perspectives, and using them to refine our approaches. Whether it's my work in education or personal projects, I find that soliciting input helps identify blind spots and areas where we might be able to improve. When receiving feedback, I try to listen attentively, ask clarifying questions, and give serious consideration to what others have said. It's essential to separate valid insights from unhelpful comments – one thing that helped me was setting ground rules for myself before responding, like writing down any concerns so I could reflect on them properly later. Incorporating feedback isn't always easy; sometimes it takes some getting used to, and I may need time to adjust to changes suggested. But ultimately, I believe that embracing an openness to growth and evolution makes us more capable individuals and professionals. By continually evaluating how we operate and adapting accordingly, we increase our chances of success and delivering better outcomes for those who rely upon us.

I think my approach is quite straightforward, really. When faced with a puzzle or problem, I try to break it down into smaller parts, identify all the key pieces of information, and look at them logically. It's like peeling an onion – once you know what makes each layer up, it becomes easier to figure out how everything fits together. Sometimes, taking a step back, having a cuppa, and clearing one's mind helps too! I'm very much a "think before acting" kind of person, which has served me well throughout my career in admin work. But, I must admit, with all these new-fangled gadgets and technology coming along, sometimes it feels like there's so many moving parts, it gets overwhelming... still, a bit of patience and persistence usually sorts me out in the end.

I think I try to take my time to read something twice before putting pen to paper or keyboard, making sure I grasp what someone has said. Sometimes taking a walk helps me process information too – being out in nature clears my mind and lets me come back to whatever task I was doing feeling more focused. And writing things down myself rather than relying solely on notes from others really helps me remember better. It seems like getting things down in black and white makes them feel more concrete, so I'm more likely to recall them later.

I think identifying areas for growth is quite simple really. It's all about being aware of your strengths and weaknesses. You know yourself best, so take some time to reflect on what makes you tick and where you feel like you're struggling a bit more. For me, it started with recognizing my limitations with technology – how sometimes I find myself getting frustrated when I'm stuck on something or need help figuring out why it isn't working properly. Now, I make sure to reach out to my grandsons whenever I have any issues, and we sort it out together. Also, since retiring, I've been making an effort to be more mindful of my health and fitness, which wasn't always a priority while working full-time. So now, I try to prioritize exercise and eating well. These little changes may seem insignificant, but they add up, and I feel much better overall because of them. It's essential to acknowledge these areas for personal development, whether big or small, and tackle them one step at a time. And remember, it's okay to ask for help along the way!

I think my strategy is quite simple, really. It's one of those "if it feels like a step forward, take it." You know, being open to new things has always been important to me, even though I have to admit, sometimes I still prefer sticking to what I'm familiar with. But, all in all, I believe having some uncertainty makes life more interesting, and there's usually something to be gained from stepping out of our comfort zones. When faced with an unfamiliar situation, I try to focus on why it might benefit me – whether it's meeting new people, acquiring new skills, or gaining fresh perspectives. And while I wouldn't say I rush into everything, once I feel the benefits outweigh any initial hesitation, I'll give it a go! After all, I've found that patience and curiosity tend to carry us much further than rushing through life ever could.

I make sure to prioritize my reading time whenever I can. Evenings are usually quite relaxing, so I like to unwind before bed by getting lost in a good book. It's become a bit of a tradition for me, really. Sometimes I'll join our local book club too; we meet once a month and discuss what we're all reading – always lovely company! And during the weekdays, even though work is done, I try to squeeze some reading into the morning while having a cuppa. Life gets busy, but making room for books feels essential to keeping myself sane. Plus, there's nothing better than escaping into another world for a bit.

I find out about new authors quite often through word-of-mouth, really. Friends and family will tell me about books they think I'd like, or we'll discuss them during our book club meetings. Occasionally, I might pick something up while browsing the local library or charity shop – those places always seem to have a treasure trove of hidden gems. And of course, sometimes my son reads reviews online before buying something, so he'll send me articles or emails recommending what he thinks is worth checking out. He knows how much I love getting recommendations! As for genres, I'm rather fond of historical fiction myself, and I tend to gravitate towards that sort of thing. But I'm happy to try other styles too, provided there's an engaging story involved.

I love a good listen! While listening to music, I usually find myself tidying away, putting books out for my grandkids to read, or maybe even taking care of some paperwork. You know, keeping busy and making sure everything is shipshape. It's become quite soothing really – a gentle rhythm helps me focus on what needs doing. Sometimes I'll take a walk outside afterwards, enjoying the fresh air and matching the beat of the music to my footsteps. Really, whatever takes my fancy! Music has always been calming and lifts my spirits, so it makes those mundane tasks more bearable.

I find them quite helpful for commutes and relaxing time, really. I like being able to multitask while still getting something done, whether it's ironing or tidying up. Audiobooks have been a lifesaver during those post-lunch dips when my energy wanes. Podcasts are great too; I usually listen to radio shows or history-related programmes – tends to wind me down before bed. I appreciate how convenient it is to absorb information on-the-go rather than having to read everything myself. It's lovely to be able to spend some time catching up on current events or educational topics without feeling rushed.

I thoroughly enjoy spending my free time tending to my vegetable patch out in the garden! There's something so satisfying about watching those seeds sprout into delicious fruits and vegetables. It's a lovely way to connect with nature and feel productive all at once. And of course, I love sharing some of my harvest with my grandkids – there's nothing quite like seeing them excitedly munching away on fresh carrots straight from our garden! In addition to gardening, I'm also rather fond of taking leisurely strolls along the nearby park paths and getting lost in a good book while enjoying the scenery. Nothing beats curling up with a cuppa and a tasty scone afterwards either! My son used to drive me to places occasionally too, which is always nice.

I love having some me-time to myself! It's lovely being able to curl up with a good book, perhaps a cuppa, and spend an hour or two getting lost in a story or learning something new. I find it so refreshing. A bit like taking a breath of fresh air, really. And, of course, tending to my garden is another great way to unwind – there's something therapeutic about watching those little seedlings grow into beautiful blooms. Sometimes, I'll take a walk along the nearby park paths while enjoying the peace and quiet; that always seems to clear my mind.

I must say, I'm a traditionalist at heart, so I tend to stick to what I know and love. However, my husband was always an adventurous eater, and we'd try out different restaurants together whenever he could persuade me. When he passed away, I thought I might lose interest in cooking, but his granddaughter has taken quite an interest in sharing her own culinary creations with us, which is lovely! Nowadays, every now and then, our grandkids come over, and they're always eager to teach me something new – usually how to make some trendy recipe they saw on one of those social... er, platforms! *laughs* It's been amusing watching them share all these exotic dishes, and sometimes they even convince me to give them a go myself. So, while I still prefer sticking to tried-and-tested favorites like roast chicken or fish pie, there have been instances where I've ventured into unfamiliar territory and enjoyed discovering new flavors. But only because someone else took the initiative to show me, really!

I think a big part of creating variety is keeping my routines simple and flexible, so I'm free to make changes whenever I like. For instance, some weeks I might focus more on gardening – there's always something new to tend to, whether it's pruning roses or planting seeds for spring. And other weeks, I'll spend more time reading historical novels or watching nature documentaries. My grandkids live nearby, too, which gives me plenty of opportunities to mix things up and try new things together. We have regular Sunday lunches, play games, or go out for walks. It's lovely having them around; it reminds me to be spontaneous! Additionally, our local community has some great initiatives and groups we participate in. The village hall hosts lectures, exhibitions, and concerts, and I love attending those. Plus, I volunteer regularly at the local library, where I meet fellow book lovers and engage in conversations about all sorts of topics. All these little bits add up to give my leisure time a bit of zest and prevent it becoming dull!

I like to jot down my thoughts and plans in a little notebook before we go out or start an activity so I know what I need to take along, who else is coming, and any specific arrangements I have made. It helps me feel more organised and ensures everyone knows what's expected of them. Sometimes, my granddaughter likes to draw pictures too, which makes our time together even more enjoyable!

I'm quite particular about keeping track of things, even though my husband used to say I was a bit too fussy! *laughs* For me, having some sort of system helps me feel more in control and focused. Over the years, I've developed a combination of digital tools – like reminders on my phone and calendar on the table where we display our family schedules – alongside paper planners and notebooks for jotting down notes and to-do lists. My daily planner is still my most trusted companion; I fill it out every morning before breakfast, listing what needs attending to throughout the day. It's amazing how much clearer your priorities become once everything's laid bare on paper. My grandkids love watching me tick off completed items – they think it makes them look busy! When it comes to specific tasks, I try breaking them down into smaller chunks so nothing feels overwhelming. A cup of tea while I go through each item, check off one thing at a time... it really does make all the difference. And let me tell you, there's something very satisfying about seeing those little boxes checked off! It might sound a bit excessive, but trust me, these simple routines have been lifesavers over the years. Now, I'd never imagine going back to being disorganized again!

Before leaving home, I make sure I have a cup of tea ready, grab my handbag and take out my keys. If I'm running late for an appointment, I might pop into the kitchen to reheat something for breakfast – usually toast with avocado or cereal. It depends on how much notice I had and what time I need to be somewhere. Sometimes I'll jot down any notes or reminders for myself on a piece of paper so I don't forget whatever task I was supposed to attend to during the day. Then I set off, either straight onto the bus or maybe popping in to say hello to the neighbours first thing.

I try to be mindful of my usage while still enjoying a warm home. You know, simple habits like turning lights out when not needed and using thick curtains during winter really make a difference. Also, I have got some draught excluders near doors and windows which helps prevent heat escaping. My grandkids love playing outside more so I try to strike a balance between keeping them cozy indoors and making sure we're not wasting too much energy. A bit of DIY effort goes a long way!

I must admit, I'm a bit hesitant about all these new-fangled gadgets. To be honest, my son is always trying to convince me to get one of those voice assistants, but I'm still not sure what to make of them. He says they're convenient, but I worry about how much information they collect and who has access to it. Still, he's been using his phone-controlled thermostat and lights, so maybe I'll give one another try eventually...but only if I really need it!

I think prioritising is quite simple really. For me, it comes down to what's most important in my personal life and making sure I'm taking care of myself while still achieving all those lovely grandkids milestones. When it gets busy, like during holidays or special occasions, flexibility is key – sometimes we have to adjust plans a bit. Generally speaking though, if there's one thing I know I must attend to first, that'll be staying fit and keeping an eye on my finances; getting enough rest so I can spend quality time with loved ones later on. If someone else needs something specific that requires my immediate assistance, I try to accommodate them too, but ultimately, family always takes precedence. With that said, even in retirement, knowing exactly how much free time I have allows me to make informed decisions that suit everyone involved.
